question: Prove or disprove that the set S defined by 1 ∈ S and 𐓒s + t ∈ S whenever s ∈ S and t ∈ S is the set of positive integers.
response: "Let’s interpret the definition carefully:* (1 \in S)* If (s \in S) and (t \in S), then (2s + t \in S)We are asked whether this generates **exactly** the set of positive integers.---## Step 1: Show everything in (S) is a positive integerStart with (1), which is a positive integer.If (s, t) are positive integers, then:[2s + t > 0]So every element generated by the rule is still a positive integer.Thus:[S \subseteq \mathbb{Z}^+]---## Step 2: Compute initial elements of (S)Start building elements:* (1 \in S)Using (s = 1, t = 1):[2(1) + 1 = 3 \Rightarrow 3 \in S]Now use combinations:* (2(1) + 3 = 5)* (2(3) + 1 = 7)So far:[S = {1, 3, 5, 7, \dots}]Try to get an even number like 2:There is no way to produce 2:* Smallest possible value is (2(1)+1 = 3)So:[2 \notin S]---## Step 3: Observe a pattern (parity)Check parity:* Start: (1) is **odd*** If (s, t) are odd:  [  2s + t = \text{even} + \text{odd} = \text{odd}  ]So every element of (S) is **odd**.Thus:[S \subseteq {\text{positive odd integers}}]---## Step 4: Show all odd positive integers are in (S)We prove by induction that every odd positive integer is in (S).### Base case:(1 \in S)### Inductive idea:Assume an odd number (k \in S). Show (k+2 \in S).Let:* (s = 1)* (t = k)Then:[2s + t = 2(1) + k = k + 2]So if (k \in S), then (k+2 \in S).Thus all odd numbers:[1, 3, 5, 7, \dots]are in (S).---## Final Conclusion* (S) contains **only odd positive integers*** (S) contains **all odd positive integers**Therefore:[S = {\text{positive odd integers}}]---## AnswerThe statement is **false**.The set (S) is **not** the set of all positive integers—it is exactly the set of **positive odd integers**."
